clusters.title: Wave of Russian strikes on Ukrainian cities kill at least 11
clusters.neutral_headline: Wave of Russian strikes on Ukrainian cities kill at least 11
bullet_summary: Russian missile and drone strikes killed several people and wounded dozens across Ukraine, authorities said, days after President Zelenskyy warned Moscow was preparing a "massive new strike
Russia attacked Ukraine overnight Tuesday, killing at least 11 people and trapping others, authorities said
Russian air strikes hit several major Ukrainian cities early on Tuesday, killing at least 11 people and wounding more than 100, authorities said

items.title: Wave of Russian strikes on Ukrainian cities kill at least 11
items.standfirst: Dozens injured and people trapped under rubble of fallen buildings after attacks on Kyiv, Dnipro and KharkivRussian air raids on major Ukrainian centres including Kyiv, Dnipro and Kharkiv have killed at least 11 people, injured dozens and left others trapped.Russia launched 73 missiles and 656 drones at Ukraine, according to the Ukrainian air force, with the main targets including Kyiv, the central city of Dnipro and the eastern cities of Poltava, Kharkiv and Zaporizhzhia. Ukrainian air defences destroyed or suppressed 40 missiles and 602 drones. Continue reading...
items.content: Dozens injured and people trapped under rubble of fallen buildings after attacks on Kyiv, Dnipro and KharkivRussian air raids on major Ukrainian centres including Kyiv, Dnipro and Kharkiv have killed at least 11 people, injured dozens and left others trapped.Russia launched 73 missiles and 656 drones at Ukraine, according to the Ukrainian air force, with the main targets including Kyiv, the central city of Dnipro and the eastern cities of Poltava, Kharkiv and Zaporizhzhia. Ukrainian air defences destroyed or suppressed 40 missiles and 602 drones. Continue reading...

items.title: Major Russian missile and drone attacks on Ukraine kill several, wound dozens
items.standfirst: Russian missile and drone strikes killed several people and wounded dozens across Ukraine, authorities said, days after President Zelenskyy warned Moscow was preparing a "massive new strike."
items.content: Russian missile and drone strikes killed several people and wounded dozens across Ukraine, authorities said, days after President Zelenskyy warned Moscow was preparing a "massive new strike."

items.title: Massive Russian attack on cities across Ukraine kills at least 10 people
items.standfirst: Rescuers are racing to find people feared trapped beneath rubble in Kyiv after the overnight missile and drone attack.
items.content: Rescuers are racing to find people feared trapped beneath rubble in Kyiv after the overnight missile and drone attack.

items.title: Russian attack on Ukraine kills at least 11 and traps others in damaged buildings
items.standfirst: Russia attacked Ukraine overnight Tuesday, killing at least 11 people and trapping others, authorities said. Residential buildings and other civilian infrastructure were damaged in eight of Kyiv's districts.
items.content: Russia attacked Ukraine overnight Tuesday, killing at least 11 people and trapping others, authorities said. Residential buildings and other civilian infrastructure were damaged in eight of Kyiv's districts.(Image credit: Efrem Lukatsky)

items.title: Russian attacks kill several people and wound more than 100 across Ukraine
items.standfirst: Russian air strikes hit several major Ukrainian cities early on Tuesday, killing at least 11 people and wounding more than 100, authorities said. Kyiv, Dnipro and Kharkiv were among the hardest hit, with residential buildings damaged and thousands of residents sheltering underground amid ongoing air raid alerts.
items.content: Russian air strikes hit several major Ukrainian cities early on Tuesday, killing at least 11 people and wounding more than 100, authorities said. Kyiv, Dnipro and Kharkiv were among the hardest hit, with residential buildings damaged and thousands of residents sheltering underground amid ongoing air raid alerts.

clusters.title: Celebration, shock and scepticism follow Colombia’s presidential election
clusters.neutral_headline: Celebration, shock and scepticism follow Colombia’s presidential election
bullet_summary: Reported by Al Jazeera, France24, Guardian World and others

items.title: Celebration, shock and scepticism follow Colombia’s presidential election
items.standfirst: Far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella beat left-wing Senator Ivan Cepeda in the first round, upending expectations.
items.content: Far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella beat left-wing Senator Ivan Cepeda in the first round, upending expectations.

items.title: Video: Presidential election has Colombia on edge
items.standfirst: Abelardo de la Espriella will face left-wing Senator Ivan Cepeda in the run-off for Colombia’s presidential election.
items.content: Abelardo de la Espriella will face left-wing Senator Ivan Cepeda in the run-off for Colombia’s presidential election.

items.title: Who is Abelardo de la Espriella, the far‑right Trump fan who could lead Colombia?
items.standfirst: Far-right defence attorney and businessman Abelardo de la Espriella topped the first round of Colombia’s presidential election Sunday, followed closely by leftist senator Ivan Cepeda. A fervent supporter of US President Donald Trump as well as the authoritarian crime crackdown of El Salvador’s President Nayib Bukele, De la Espriella has promised to wage an uncompromising war against the splintered armed groups that continue to hold out in the country’s hinterlands.
items.content: Far-right defence attorney and businessman Abelardo de la Espriella topped the first round of Colombia’s presidential election Sunday, followed closely by leftist senator Ivan Cepeda. A fervent supporter of US President Donald Trump as well as the authoritarian crime crackdown of El Salvador’s President Nayib Bukele, De la Espriella has promised to wage an uncompromising war against the splintered armed groups that continue to hold out in the country’s hinterlands.

items.title: Trump admirer’s surprise first-round win is a blow to Colombia’s traditional conservatives
items.standfirst: Success of far-right presidential candidate, Abelardo de la Espriella, suggests some voters are ‘fed up with politics’The far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella and the leftwing senator Iván Cepeda have just under three weeks to compete for the roughly 3.6m votes that did not go to either of them in the first round of Colombia’s presidential election.That is no insignificant number, given that De la Espriella’s lead over Cepeda amounted to little more than 670,000 votes – 43.7% to 40.9%. Continue reading...
items.content: Success of far-right presidential candidate, Abelardo de la Espriella, suggests some voters are ‘fed up with politics’The far-right lawyer Abelardo de la Espriella and the leftwing senator Iván Cepeda have just under three weeks to compete for the roughly 3.6m votes that did not go to either of them in the first round of Colombia’s presidential election.That is no insignificant number, given that De la Espriella’s lead over Cepeda amounted to little more than 670,000 votes – 43.7% to 40.9%. Continue reading...

items.title: Colombia’s far-right presidential candidate De la Espriella wins first round of vote ahead of runoff
items.standfirst: Lawyer and Trump admirer has risen rapidly in the polls and will face Iván Cepeda in election runoff in three weeksA far-right lawyer and Donald Trump admirer will go head-to-head against leftwing senator Iván Cepeda in the race to be Colombia’s next president after he won a surprise victory in the first round of voting.With 100% of ballots counted, the outsider Abelardo de la Espriella secured 43.7% of the vote – just over 10.3m votes – compared with 40.9% (about 9.6m votes) for Cepeda, a philosopher and human rights activist who has served as a senator since 2014 and is backed by the current leftwing president, Gustavo Petro. Continue reading...
items.content: Lawyer and Trump admirer has risen rapidly in the polls and will face Iván Cepeda in election runoff in three weeksA far-right lawyer and Donald Trump admirer will go head-to-head against leftwing senator Iván Cepeda in the race to be Colombia’s next president after he won a surprise victory in the first round of voting.With 100% of ballots counted, the outsider Abelardo de la Espriella secured 43.7% of the vote – just over 10.3m votes – compared with 40.9% (about 9.6m votes) for Cepeda, a philosopher and human rights activist who has served as a senator since 2014 and is backed by the current leftwing president, Gustavo Petro. Continue reading...

items.title: Colombia’s Petro sows doubt about election as favored successor heads to runoff against pro-Trump rival
items.standfirst: Colombia’s outgoing president sowed doubt Sunday about his country’s elections, which showed his preferred candidate, Iván Cepeda, headed to a runoff next month against right-wing opponent Abelardo de la Espriella
items.content: Colombia’s outgoing president sowed doubt Sunday about his country’s elections, which showed his preferred candidate, Iván Cepeda, headed to a runoff next month against right-wing opponent Abelardo de la Espriella

items.title: Colombia presidential runoff pits leftist senator against pro-Trump rival
items.standfirst: Left-wing senator Iván Cepeda will face Trump admirer Abelardo de la Espriella at the final ballot on 21 June.
items.content: Left-wing senator Iván Cepeda will face Trump admirer Abelardo de la Espriella at the final ballot on 21 June.

items.title: Colombia goes to polls in election pitting outgoing leader’s ally against pro-Trump candidates
items.standfirst: Ballots are being cast in the first round of the South American nation’s presidential electionsColombians are casting ballots in the first round of the South American nation’s presidential election, choosing between candidates with radically diverging visions for the future of peace in a country haunted by decades of armed conflict.The vote on Sunday, seen as a referendum on outgoing President Gustavo Petro’s policies, comes 10 years after Colombia signed a historic peace pact with guerrillas of the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (Farc). Continue reading...
items.content: Ballots are being cast in the first round of the South American nation’s presidential electionsColombians are casting ballots in the first round of the South American nation’s presidential election, choosing between candidates with radically diverging visions for the future of peace in a country haunted by decades of armed conflict.The vote on Sunday, seen as a referendum on outgoing President Gustavo Petro’s policies, comes 10 years after Colombia signed a historic peace pact with guerrillas of the Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (Farc). Continue reading...

clusters.neutral_headline: Denmark's Mette Frederiksen to form government after months of negotiations

items.title: Denmark's Mette Frederiksen to form government after months of negotiations
items.standfirst: The leader of Denmark's Social Democratic Party is forming a centre-left coalition minority government, giving her a third term as prime minister.
items.content: The leader of Denmark's Social Democratic Party is forming a centre-left coalition minority government, giving her a third term as prime minister.

items.title: Danish PM Frederiksen secures third term with new coalition government
items.standfirst: Denmark’s Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen announced on Monday that she has formed a new left-leaning minority coalition after months of negotiations following inconclusive elections in March. The agreement secures her third term in office, bringing together four parties that hold a slim parliamentary majority.
items.content: Denmark’s Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen announced on Monday that she has formed a new left-leaning minority coalition after months of negotiations following inconclusive elections in March. The agreement secures her third term in office, bringing together four parties that hold a slim parliamentary majority.

items.title: Denmark’s Mette Frederiksen agrees centre-left coalition after months of uncertainty
items.standfirst: Social Democratic leader secures third consecutive term as prime minister amid crisis in ties with US over GreenlandDenmark’s Social Democratic leader, Mette Frederiksen, said on Monday she had agreed to form a centre-left coalition government, maintaining her grip on power amid a crisis in ties with Donald Trump over Greenland.The deal to form a minority cabinet gives Frederiksen a third consecutive term as prime minister, ending months of uncertainty after a March election in which 12 parties won seats in the Danish parliament. Continue reading...
items.content: Social Democratic leader secures third consecutive term as prime minister amid crisis in ties with US over GreenlandDenmark’s Social Democratic leader, Mette Frederiksen, said on Monday she had agreed to form a centre-left coalition government, maintaining her grip on power amid a crisis in ties with Donald Trump over Greenland.The deal to form a minority cabinet gives Frederiksen a third consecutive term as prime minister, ending months of uncertainty after a March election in which 12 parties won seats in the Danish parliament. Continue reading...

clusters.title: Middle East war live: Israeli military intercepts projectiles from Lebanon
clusters.neutral_headline: Trump says Hezbollah and Israel have agreed to ‘stop all shooting’
bullet_summary: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Monday he told US President Donald Trump that his country would strike Beirut if Hezbollah doesn't stop attacking Israel
Earlier, Trump had said that Israel and Hezbollah had agreed to dial back fighting
The US president said in a social media post that he spoke to Israel’s prime minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, and representatives of Hezbollah and both agreed that “all shooting will stop”
The Iranian foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, said: “The ceasefire between Iran and the US is unequivocally a ceasefire on all fronts, including in Lebanon

items.title: Middle East war live: Israeli military intercepts projectiles from Lebanon
items.standfirst: Tensions along the Israel-Lebanon border continued early on Tuesday as Israeli air defences intercepted incoming projectiles, despite US President Donald Trump’s claim that he had brokered an end to fighting between Israel and Hezbollah. Follow our liveblog for the latest updates.
items.content: Tensions along the Israel-Lebanon border continued early on Tuesday as Israeli air defences intercepted incoming projectiles, despite US President Donald Trump’s claim that he had brokered an end to fighting between Israel and Hezbollah. Follow our liveblog for the latest updates.

items.title: US says Israel and Hezbollah agree to dial back fighting as tensions escalate
items.standfirst: Tensions between Israel and Hezbollah intensified on Monday, after US President Donald Trump said the two sides had agreed to dial back fighting following talks with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and indirect communication with Hezbollah.
items.content: Tensions between Israel and Hezbollah intensified on Monday, after US President Donald Trump said the two sides had agreed to dial back fighting following talks with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu and indirect communication with Hezbollah.

items.title: 'Healthy consensus in Israel' on pursuing Lebanon campaign, expert says
items.standfirst: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Monday he told US President Donald Trump that his country would strike Beirut if Hezbollah doesn't stop attacking Israel. Earlier, Trump had said that Israel and Hezbollah had agreed to dial back fighting. Speaking with FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney, Bilal Y. Saab, Senior Managing Director of TRENDS Research and Advisory U.S., explains that "there's a healthy political consensus in Israel about the merits and desirability of the military campaign in Lebanon".
items.content: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said on Monday he told US President Donald Trump that his country would strike Beirut if Hezbollah doesn't stop attacking Israel. Earlier, Trump had said that Israel and Hezbollah had agreed to dial back fighting. Speaking with FRANCE 24's Sharon Gaffney, Bilal Y. Saab, Senior Managing Director of TRENDS Research and Advisory U.S., explains that "there's a healthy political consensus in Israel about the merits and desirability of the military campaign in Lebanon".

items.title: Trump says Hezbollah and Israel have agreed to ‘stop all shooting’
items.standfirst: Declaration comes after Iran had pulled out of peace talks in protest at Israeli offensive in LebanonDonald Trump has said Hezbollah and Israel have agreed to mutual de-escalation and to scale back fighting, seemingly averting an Israeli strike on Beirut and the potential collapse of ceasefire talks with Iran.The US president said in a social media post that he spoke to Israel’s prime minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, and representatives of Hezbollah and both agreed that “all shooting will stop”. Continue reading...
items.content: Declaration comes after Iran had pulled out of peace talks in protest at Israeli offensive in LebanonDonald Trump has said Hezbollah and Israel have agreed to mutual de-escalation and to scale back fighting, seemingly averting an Israeli strike on Beirut and the potential collapse of ceasefire talks with Iran.The US president said in a social media post that he spoke to Israel’s prime minister, Benjamin Netanyahu, and representatives of Hezbollah and both agreed that “all shooting will stop”. Continue reading...

items.title: Trump says Israel and Hezbollah have agreed to dial back fighting
items.standfirst: US President Donald Trump said Monday he had persuaded Israel and Hezbollah to de-escalate, with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu agreeing not to send troops to southern Beirut and the Lebanese militant group promising to stop attacks. Reporting from Jerusalem, FRANCE 24's Noga Tarnopolsky says that, if true, the move represents a "major coup" for both Hezbollah and Iran.
items.content: US President Donald Trump said Monday he had persuaded Israel and Hezbollah to de-escalate, with Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu agreeing not to send troops to southern Beirut and the Lebanese militant group promising to stop attacks. Reporting from Jerusalem, FRANCE 24's Noga Tarnopolsky says that, if true, the move represents a "major coup" for both Hezbollah and Iran.

items.title: Iran threatens to suspend peace talks after ‘violation of ceasefire’ in Lebanon
items.standfirst: Tehran says it holds Israel and US responsible, while Trump says ‘going silent’ on negotiations ‘would be very good’Iran has indicated it will suspend peace talks with the US in protest against Israel’s offensive in Lebanon, threatening the collapse of negotiations with Washington as the two sides skirmished amid a faltering ceasefire.The Iranian foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, said: “The ceasefire between Iran and the US is unequivocally a ceasefire on all fronts, including in Lebanon. Its violation on one front is a violation of the ceasefire on all fronts. The US and Israel are responsible for the consequences of any violation.” Continue reading...
items.content: Tehran says it holds Israel and US responsible, while Trump says ‘going silent’ on negotiations ‘would be very good’Iran has indicated it will suspend peace talks with the US in protest against Israel’s offensive in Lebanon, threatening the collapse of negotiations with Washington as the two sides skirmished amid a faltering ceasefire.The Iranian foreign minister, Abbas Araghchi, said: “The ceasefire between Iran and the US is unequivocally a ceasefire on all fronts, including in Lebanon. Its violation on one front is a violation of the ceasefire on all fronts. The US and Israel are responsible for the consequences of any violation.” Continue reading...

bullet_summary: 8bn fund to compensate his allies, a person familiar with his thinking said on Monday, as the justice department paused the program to comply with a court order
Trump’s “anti-weaponization” fund has faced legal setbacks since it was announced two weeks ago
US President Donald Trump was said to be reconsidering a controversial $1

items.title: Trump reportedly mulling retreat from $1.8bn ‘anti-weaponization’ fund
items.standfirst: Democrats decry president’s ‘most brazen act of self-dealing yet’ and vow to challenge fund in CongressDonald Trump is reconsidering whether to keep pressing for a $1.8bn fund to compensate his allies, a person familiar with his thinking said on Monday, as the justice department paused the program to comply with a court order.Trump’s “anti-weaponization” fund has faced legal setbacks since it was announced two weeks ago. The idea has also faced a mounting political backlash from Republicans concerned by a lack of oversight and the possibility of payouts to participants in the January 6 2021 riot at the US Capitol. Continue reading...
items.content: Democrats decry president’s ‘most brazen act of self-dealing yet’ and vow to challenge fund in CongressDonald Trump is reconsidering whether to keep pressing for a $1.8bn fund to compensate his allies, a person familiar with his thinking said on Monday, as the justice department paused the program to comply with a court order.Trump’s “anti-weaponization” fund has faced legal setbacks since it was announced two weeks ago. The idea has also faced a mounting political backlash from Republicans concerned by a lack of oversight and the possibility of payouts to participants in the January 6 2021 riot at the US Capitol. Continue reading...

items.title: Trump reconsiders $1.8 billion ally compensation fund amid Republican backlash
items.standfirst: US President Donald Trump was said to be reconsidering a controversial $1.8 billion compensation fund on Monday, created as part of a settlement with the IRS, amid legal challenges and growing opposition from Republicans. The Justice Department has paused implementation following a court order, raising doubts about the fund's future.
items.content: US President Donald Trump was said to be reconsidering a controversial $1.8 billion compensation fund on Monday, created as part of a settlement with the IRS, amid legal challenges and growing opposition from Republicans. The Justice Department has paused implementation following a court order, raising doubts about the fund's future.
